name: Lavender
description: A fragrant, drought-tolerant perennial shrub native to the Mediterranean. Produces striking purple flower spikes beloved by bees and butterflies. Widely used in cooking, aromatherapy, and natural medicine.
soilPreference: Well-drained, slightly alkaline (pH 6.5–7.5), sandy or loamy
culinaryUses: Flavoring for baked goods, teas, and syrups
gardeningTips: Prune after flowering to maintain shape. Avoid overwatering — root rot is the #1 killer. Mulch in colder zones.

name: Basil
description: An essential culinary herb with a sweet, clove-like aroma. Thrives in warm, sunny conditions. Excellent companion plant for tomatoes, repelling pests and potentially improving fruit flavor.
gardeningTips: Pinch flower buds to extend leaf production. Plant after last frost. Keep away from cold drafts indoors.

name: Coneflower (Echinacea)
description: A striking native North American prairie wildflower with prominent spiky seed heads. One of the most studied medicinal plants in the world, widely used to support immune function.
water: Low to moderate — very drought tolerant
gardeningTips: Leave seed heads standing in winter — birds love them. Divide clumps every 3–4 years. Self-seeds prolifically.

name: Comfrey
description: A vigorous perennial herb with deep taproots that mine minerals from subsoil. Leaves are prized as a dynamic accumulator for composting and as a natural fertilizer mulch (chop-and-drop).
soilPreference: Rich, moist, loamy (pH 6.0–7.0) — tolerates poor soils
culinaryUses: Leaves (small amounts) — use cautiously due to pyrrolizidine alkaloids
medicinalUses: Wound healing (topical), bruise treatment, bone healing support
gardeningTips: Plant once — very hard to remove. Ideal as orchard understory. Chop leaves 3–5x per season as mulch. Rich in potassium, nitrogen, phosphorus.
propagation: Root cuttings or division — root fragments grow readily

name: Yarrow
description: Feathery-leaved perennial with flat-topped clusters of tiny flowers. One of the oldest medicinal plants known, used by indigenous peoples across the Northern Hemisphere. Excellent companion that attracts beneficial insects.
culinaryUses: Young leaves in salads, dried flowers in teas
medicinalUses: Wound healing, fever reduction, anti-inflammatory, digestive tonic
gardeningTips: Spreads by rhizomes — plant in a spot where spreading is OK or use a root barrier. Divide every 2–3 years. Attracts ladybugs and lacewings.

name: Elderberry
description: A fast-growing native shrub/small tree producing large flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers followed by dark purple-black berries. Both flowers and berries are valued for immune-supporting properties.
soilPreference: Moist, fertile, well-drained (pH 5.5–6.5) — tolerates wet conditions
culinaryUses: Elderflower cordial, elderberry syrup, jams, wine
medicinalUses: Antiviral, immune stimulant, flu relief, elderberry syrup
gardeningTips: Plant two varieties for better fruit set. Prune old canes to ground each spring. Raw berries are mildly toxic — always cook before consuming.

name: Rosemary
description: An intensely aromatic Mediterranean shrub with needle-like leaves. One of the most essential culinary herbs, also highly valued in traditional medicine and as a landscape plant in warm climates.
medicinalUses: Memory and cognition support, antioxidant, circulation
gardeningTips: Prune lightly after blooming — never cut into old wood. Excellent in containers. Bring indoors in zone 6 or colder.
propagation: Stem cuttings (easiest), layering, seed (slow to germinate)

name: Stinging Nettle
description: A common 'weed' that is one of nature's most nutritious plants. Despite its stinging hairs (neutralized by cooking or drying), nettles are prized as a food, medicine, fiber plant, and garden liquid fertilizer.
soilPreference: Rich, moist, nitrogen-rich soil (pH 5.5–7.0) — indicator of fertile ground
culinaryUses: Cooked as greens (like spinach), nettle soup, pesto, nettle tea
medicinalUses: Iron-rich, anti-inflammatory, BPH support, allergy relief
gardeningTips: Wear gloves when harvesting. Young spring tips are best. Steep leaves in water for a powerful nitrogen-rich liquid fertilizer. Great habitat plant for butterflies.

name: Calendula (Pot Marigold)
description: Bright orange and yellow daisy-like flowers that bloom almost continuously from spring to frost. One of the most useful plants in the garden — edible, medicinal, and an excellent companion pest deterrent.
culinaryUses: Petals in salads, rice, soups as saffron substitute
gardeningTips: Deadhead regularly for continuous bloom. Self-seeds freely. Excellent for cut flowers. Harvest petals in the morning when fully open.
propagation: Direct seed in spring (or fall in mild climates)

name: Dandelion
description: The most misunderstood garden plant. Dandelions are nutritional powerhouses with edible leaves, flowers, and roots. Their deep taproots break up compacted soil and bring up minerals from deep layers.
soilPreference: Tolerates almost any soil — often indicates compaction or low calcium
culinaryUses: Young leaves in salads, flowers in wine/fritters, roasted root as coffee substitute
medicinalUses: Liver and digestive support, diuretic (potassium-sparing), rich in vitamins A, C, K
gardeningTips: Allow some to bloom — early-season food for pollinators. The taproot is a natural aerator. Harvest young spring leaves before flowering for best flavor.

name: Chamomile (German)
description: Delicate, apple-scented annual producing masses of small daisy flowers. One of the most popular medicinal herbs globally, best known for its calming properties. Self-seeds prolifically once established.
medicinalUses: Calming, sleep aid, digestive, anti-inflammatory, skin soothing
gardeningTips: Scatter seed on bare soil in fall or early spring — don't cover, it needs light to germinate. Pick flowers when petals begin to turn downward.

name: Wild Garlic (Ramps)
description: One of the most prized spring forage plants in temperate forests. Broad, smooth leaves carpet woodland floors in early spring with an unmistakable garlic scent. Every part is edible.
soilPreference: Rich, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil (pH 5.0–7.0)
culinaryUses: Leaves in pesto, soups, salads; bulbs like garlic; flowers in salads
medicinalUses: Cardiovascular support, antibacterial, similar to garlic medicinally
gardeningTips: IMPORTANT: Do not confuse with Lily of the Valley or Lords and Ladies (both toxic) — always verify garlic scent before eating. Plant bulbs in fall in woodland or shaded garden.
propagation: Bulb offsets, seed (2+ years to flowering)

name: Black-Eyed Susan
description: A cheerful native prairie wildflower with golden-yellow petals and a dark chocolate-brown center. Extremely tough, long-blooming, and beloved by pollinators. One of the best plants for low-maintenance naturalized meadows.
soilPreference: Average, well-drained (pH 6.0–7.0) — tolerates poor, dry soils
medicinalUses: Minor — roots used in some Native American traditions
gardeningTips: Leave seed heads for winter bird interest. Self-seeds freely. Short-lived perennial that naturalizes well in meadows. Plant with native grasses for stunning prairie effect.
propagation: Seed (direct sow spring or fall), division

name: Valerian
description: A tall, architectural perennial with clusters of tiny pink-white flowers adored by butterflies and cats. The root is one of the oldest sleep and anxiety remedies known to Western herbalism.
medicinalUses: Sleep aid, anxiety reduction, muscle relaxant, GABA modulator
gardeningTips: Attracts earthworms when planted in vegetable beds — excellent soil health indicator. Harvest roots in fall of year 2+. Flowers attract butterflies and bees.

name: Borage
description: A robust annual with striking star-shaped blue flowers that are among the few truly blue edible flowers. An excellent companion plant that deters pests, attracts bees, and improves flavor of nearby crops.
culinaryUses: Flowers in salads, cocktails (freeze in ice cubes), young leaves with cucumber flavor
medicinalUses: Adrenal support, anti-inflammatory, GLA-rich seed oil
gardeningTips: Sow directly — doesn't transplant well. Self-seeds vigorously. Young leaves have a hairy texture — older leaves may irritate sensitive skin. Extremely attractive to bumblebees.
